critic_raw:
This transformer vs. logistic regression genomic disease susceptibility experiment has a serious label construction problem and a preprocessing leakage issue. The label construction rule — positive if family history AND ≥3 known disease-associated SNPs, negative if no family history AND <2 disease SNPs — uses a tautological criterion. A model predicting this label is partly predicting whether individuals carry GWAS-associated SNPs, which is information already encoded in the input features (the 500,000 SNPs). This creates a direct pathway from input features to label that any sufficiently expressive model will exploit, inflating AUC estimates beyond what reflects true disease susceptibility prediction.

Second, the SNP filtering and imputation pipeline is described as being 'fit on the combined train, validation, and test sets, ensuring stability across data splits.' This is preprocessing leakage: MAF computation on the full dataset uses test set allele frequencies to determine which SNPs to include in training. In deployment, the SNP panel would be determined from training data only. Similarly, mean imputation stratified by population across the entire dataset uses test set genotypes to impute training set missing values.

Third, the feature space (50,000-100,000 SNPs, 200,000 samples) puts the transformer in a severely high-dimensional regime where standard transformer architectures are not appropriate. The design acknowledges the 'high-dimensional, sparse feature regime' as a design challenge but does not address how position embeddings or attention would be meaningful across 50,000-100,000 independently ordered SNP positions without genomic positional encoding.

Finally, population stratification — a known major confounder in GWAS — is handled via stratified splits but not via explicit covariate control (e.g., including top genomic principal components as features). Without principal component adjustment, both models may learn ancestry-phenotype confounds despite balanced ancestry representation.
all_issues_raised: Circular label construction: the positive label criterion includes carrying GWAS-associated SNPs, which are encoded in the input features — any model learns to predict its own label criterion rather than true disease susceptibility
Preprocessing leakage: SNP filtering (MAF threshold), imputation, and normalization all computed on the combined dataset including validation and test sets
Architectural mismatch: standard transformer position embeddings are not appropriate for 50,000-100,000 unordered SNP features — genomic positional encoding or specialized architectures are required
Population stratification not adjusted via principal component covariates — balanced ancestry representation in splits is necessary but not sufficient to prevent ancestry-phenotype confounding

No intermediate baseline (e.g., LASSO, random forest, or polygenic risk score) between logistic regression and transformer — the comparison skips over interpretable nonlinear models relevant to the genomic literature
Critical preprocessing leakage: SNP filtering, imputation, and normalization are explicitly fit on the combined train, validation, and test sets — all feature engineering steps incorporate test data

Label construction confound: the proxy label combines GWAS-known SNPs with family history, meaning the transformer may learn to reproduce GWAS associations rather than discover independent susceptibility signals

Sample size inconsistency: the design claims ~200,000 samples from the 1000 Genomes Project, but the 1000 Genomes Project contains approximately 2,500 samples — this discrepancy undermines confidence in the experimental setup

Familial relatedness leakage: 1000 Genomes contains related individuals; ancestry-group stratification does not prevent relatives from appearing in both train and test sets, creating genetic similarity leakage

Extreme dimensionality challenge unaddressed: even at the stated smaller scale (~2,500 samples, 50,000–100,000 features), the feature-to-sample ratio makes transformer training highly prone to overfitting despite the described regularization

Data leakage: SNP filtering (MAF threshold), imputation means, and z-score normalization all computed on combined train+validation+test dataset, encoding test-set genetic structure into preprocessing decisions

Circular label definition: positive label partially determined by known disease-associated SNPs (GWAS hits), which are the same type of features used in model training — this inflates apparent performance by aligning labels with features
Exclusion of ambiguous samples creates an artificially bimodal, clean dataset that does not represent the true continuum of disease susceptibility, overstating discriminative performance

1000 Genomes Project is European-biased; if headline AUC is computed primarily on the majority ancestry group, it overstates performance for underrepresented populations, even with per-ancestry secondary analyses

Transformer attention mechanism on 50,000+ independent SNPs (not ordered by genomic position) may not capture biologically meaningful epistatic interactions — SNP ordering and positional encoding choices critically affect whether the attention is biologically interpretable or arbitrary
